full_response:
Let  

X t  := length of the line at the end of minute t, t = 0,1,2,…  

and fix the balking threshold n ≥ 1 and the arrival probability 0 < p < 1.
As was done in part (d) we view (X t ) as a discrete-time Markov chain on the
vertex set  

S = {0,1,2,…,n,n+1}.                   (1)

(The line can never become longer than n+1 : the only way customers are
added is “+2” after one service, so the largest length that can appear is
n–1 + 2 = n+1, and as soon as the length is ≥ n the next arrival is refused.)

Transitions.  
Let k∈S.

• If k = 0:
        P(0→0)=1–p, P(0→2)=p.  
• If 1 ≤ k ≤ n–1:
        P(k→k–1)=1–p, P(k→k+1)=p.  
• If k = n:
        P(n→n–1)=1–p, P(n→n+1)=p.  
• If k = n+1:
        P(n+1→n)=1.                                   (2)

Part (d) showed that when p < 1/2 this chain possesses the stationary
distribution  

π(k)=C·(2p) k / (1–p) k       for 0≤k≤n, π(n+1)=C·(2p) n+1 /(1–p) n ,  
with the appropriate normalising constant C.  
(For p ≥ 1/2 no normalisable stationary distribution exists.)

We are asked whether, whenever this π exists, the distribution of X t
always converges to π, no matter how the chain is started.

Claim.  
For every 0 < p < 1/2 and every initial state X 0 ,
the distribution of X t converges to the stationary distribution π found in
part (d):

  lim t→∞  P(X t = k | X 0 = i)  =  π(k) for all i,k ∈ S.          (3)

Proof.

1.  Irreducibility.  
    For any states i,k ∈ S we can move from i down to 0 by repeatedly
    choosing the “no-arrival’’ outcome (probability (1–p) > 0 each time);
    that takes exactly i steps and has probability (1–p) i > 0.  
    From 0 we can reach any k ≤ n+1 by a suitable sequence of
    “+2’’ (probability p) and “–1’’ (probability 1–p) moves,
    each of which has positive probability.  
    Hence every state communicates with every other; the chain is
    irreducible.

2.  Aperiodicity.  
    State 0 has a self–loop:
          P(0→0)=1–p>0.                         (4)
    Therefore the period of state 0 is 1.
    Because the chain is irreducible, every state must then have period 1;
    the chain is aperiodic.

3.  Positive recurrence.  
    The state space S is finite.  Every finite irreducible Markov chain is
    positive recurrent, so the stationary distribution π is unique.

4.  Convergence.  
    A fundamental theorem for Markov chains (sometimes called the
    Ergodic Theorem) states:

      If a discrete–time Markov chain is irreducible, aperiodic and
      positive recurrent, then for every pair of states i, k
          lim t→∞  P(X t = k | X 0 = i) = π(k).          (5)

    Conditions irreducible + aperiodic + positive recurrent have just been
    verified, so (5) holds and (3) is proved.

Consequently, whenever the stationary distribution of part (d) exists
(i.e. whenever p < 1/2), the random walk on G started from
any initial distribution converges to that stationary distribution.
When p ≥ 1/2 the stationary distribution does not exist, so convergence
to it is impossible.

\boxed{%
Yes.  For 0<p<1/2 the chain on \{0,1,\dots ,n+1\} is finite,
irreducible, and aperiodic (state 0 has a self-loop); hence it is ergodic
and the distribution of the line length converges to the unique stationary
distribution derived in part (d), no matter how the process is started.
For p\ge 1/2 that stationary distribution does not exist, so the question
is moot.} 

Confidence: 0.33

Yes.  For 0<p<1/2 the chain on \{0,1,\dots ,n+1\} is finite,
irreducible, and aperiodic (state 0 has a self-loop); hence it is ergodic
and the distribution of the line length converges to the unique stationary
distribution derived in part (d), no matter how the process is started.
For p\ge 1/2 that stationary distribution does not exist, so the question
is moot.
